summary:
The End of the Eighty Years' War (1648 AD) — the formal conclusion of the [[event:2406|Dutch Revolt]] against Spanish Habsburg rule — came with the signing of the Treaty of Münster as part of the broader [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]], in which Spain formally recognized the sovereign independence of the Dutch Republic (the Republic of the Seven United Netherlands) after 80 years of intermittent warfare. Spain's recognition, negotiated in the same Westphalian congress that ended the [[event:2328|Thirty Years' War]], acknowledged a geopolitical reality that had existed for decades: the Dutch Republic had for generations functioned as an effectively independent state, built a global commercial empire, and successfully defended itself against the world's most powerful military empire.

The peace's specific terms confirmed Dutch control of the southern approaches to the Rhine (maintaining the permanent closure of the Scheldt River to navigation, strangling the commercial rival port of Antwerp — Spain's best remaining gift to Dutch merchants, as it eliminated competition from Antwerp). The Dutch Republic's independence enabled the full expression of the [[event:2326|Dutch Golden Age]] commercial and cultural flowering that had already been underway for decades. Spain's acceptance of defeat represented a turning point in its European hegemony: after 1648, Spain would no longer be the dominant force in European affairs, replaced by France as the continent's most powerful state. The Eighty Years' War's legacy for the Netherlands was profound: the experience of successful resistance against imperial oppression became a founding myth of Dutch national identity, while the commercial prosperity that independence enabled transformed the United Provinces into one of history's most extraordinary small-state success stories.
